bhamati-advaita.short_description: Advaita lineage descending from Maṇḍana through Vāchaspati holding the jīva as the locus of avidyā.

vivarana-advaita.short_description: Advaita lineage descending from Padmapāda through Prakāśātman holding Brahman as both āśraya and viṣaya of avidyā.

suresvara-line.short_description: The jñāna-only line from Sureśvara through Sarvajñātman that becomes the Brahman-as-locus-of-avidyā stream feeding Vivaraṇa.

proto-advaita.short_description: Pre-Śaṅkara identity-leaning currents (Gauḍapāda's ajāti-vāda; Sundara-Pāṇḍya's verses).

advaita-modern-revival.short_description: Late-modern Advaita continuity through the Maṭha-pontifical tradition into the twentieth century.

advaita-school-defining.short_description: Śaṅkara's own bhāṣya-corpus, the school's definitional core before the Bhāmatī/Vivaraṇa fork.

advaita-trans-school-synthesist.short_description: Late-medieval Advaitins (Appayya) who synthesize across schools, especially with Śaiva-Advaita.

advaita-vivarana-pedagogical.short_description: The pāṭhaśālā-curriculum stream (Dharmarāja's Vedānta-Paribhāṣā) crystallizing Advaita epistemology for teaching.

vadakalai.short_description: Northern Śrīvaiṣṇava school stemming from Vedānta Deśika; emphasizes Sanskrit śāstra and a monkey-hold (markaṭa-nyāya) view of grace.

tenkalai.short_description: Southern Śrīvaiṣṇava school stemming from Pillai Lokācārya; emphasizes Tamil hymnody and a cat-hold (mārjāra-nyāya) view of grace and total surrender.

vishishtadvaita-pancaratra-grounded.short_description: Earliest systematizing Śrīvaiṣṇava stratum (Nāthamuni) bridging Tamil Āḻvār hymnody and Sanskrit philosophy with Pāñcarātra ritual grounding.

vishishtadvaita-proto-systematic.short_description: Yāmuna's pre-Rāmānuja systematization of Śrīvaiṣṇava Vedānta and defense of Pāñcarātra.

vishishtadvaita-school-defining.short_description: Rāmānuja's own corpus that defines the school via the sapta-vidhā-anupapatti and śarīra-śarīri-bhāva.

vishishtadvaita-scholastic.short_description: Sub-commentarial scholasticism (Sudarśana Sūri's Śruta-Prakāśikā) that makes Rāmānuja's compressed dialectic readable.

tattvavada.short_description: Madhva's own school-defining position: pañca-bheda and svatantra/paratantra as the structure of reality.

dvaita-scholastic.short_description: The Jayatīrtha sub-commentary tradition that systematizes Madhva's prose.

navya-dvaita.short_description: The dialectical Dvaita of Vyāsatīrtha and Vijayīndra engaging Navya-Nyāya and Advaita at the highest formal level.

dvaita-scholastic-devotional.short_description: Rāghavendra's combination of high scholasticism with the living Mantrālaya devotional tradition.

aupadhika-bhedabheda.short_description: Bhāskara's adventitious-condition Bhedābheda: Brahman appears differentiated owing to upādhis, not by intrinsic nature.

svabhavika-bhedabheda.short_description: Nimbārka's natural-difference-and-non-difference: Brahman is intrinsically (not adventitiously) both same-as and distinct-from souls and world.

proto-bhedabheda.short_description: Pre-Śaṅkara bhedābheda current attested only through citations (Bhartṛprapañca, Āśmarathya).

bhedabheda-transitional.short_description: Yādava-Prakāśa's bhedābheda variant out of which Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita emerged by polemical break.

bhedabheda-dvaitadvaita-scholastic.short_description: Śrīnivāsa's standard sub-commentary on Nimbārka, the Vedānta-Kaustubha, that fixes how the school is read in tradition.

gaudiya-school-founding.short_description: Caitanya's school-founding presence, propagated more by example and disciples than by his own treatise-writing.

gaudiya-rasa-theology.short_description: Rūpa Gosvāmī's systematic application of rasa-theory to bhakti, codifying Gauḍīya devotional aesthetics.

gaudiya.short_description: The Gauḍīya stream (Sanātana, Viśvanātha) consolidating practice, hermeneutics, and devotional manuals.

gaudiya-systematic-philosophy.short_description: Jīva Gosvāmī's Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha gives the Gauḍīya school its systematic philosophical exposition.

gaudiya-formal-vedantic.short_description: Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa's Govinda-Bhāṣya formally seats the Caitanya tradition among the bhāṣyakāras.

gaudiya-modern.short_description: Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura's late-colonial Vedāntic reformulation of the Gauḍīya tradition.

shuddhadvaita-school-defining.short_description: Vallabha's pure non-dualism without māyā, defending Brahman-world identity through avikṛta-pariṇāma.

pushti-marga.short_description: The path of grace: Vallabha's living tradition consolidated by Viṭṭhalanātha as a sectarian devotional community.

pushti-marga-scholastic.short_description: Puruṣottama's standard scholastic apparatus on Vallabha's Aṇu-Bhāṣya, the form Puṣṭimārga is studied in today.

avibhaga-advaita.short_description: Vijñānabhikṣu's harmonizing Vedānta + Sāṃkhya + Yoga thesis: Brahman and world are inseparable (avibhāga) yet not numerically identical.

proto-vedanta-sutra-author.short_description: Bādarāyaṇa, the (multi-layered) author of the Brahma-Sūtra, the spine that every later school must commentate.

proto-vedanta-citation-only.short_description: Pre-Bādarāyaṇa voices known only by name within the Brahma-Sūtra (Auḍulomi, Āśmarathya, Kāśakṛtsna).

proto-vedanta-claimed-by-vishishtadvaita.short_description: Lost-corpus figure (Bodhāyana the Vṛttikāra) whose authority the Śrīvaiṣṇava school claims as restored by Rāmānuja.

proto-vedanta-claimed-by-both.display_name: Claimed by both Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita
proto-vedanta-claimed-by-both.short_description: Upavarṣa, cited respectfully by both Śaṅkara and Rāmānuja, contested between the schools.

proto-vedanta-bhavana-jnana-hybrid.short_description: Brahmadatta, a pre-Śaṅkara teacher of knowledge-as-bhāvanā cited by Sureśvara and Vāchaspati.

proto-advaita-asmarathya-leaning.short_description: Āśmarathya's apparent proto-bhedābheda position cited approvingly by Bādarāyaṇa.

proto-advaita-kasakrtsna-leaning.short_description: Kāśakṛtsna's apparent proto-identity-thesis cited at Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.22.

trika-recognition-school-founding.short_description: Utpaladeva's Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā: liberation as recognition of the always-already-Śiva nature of awareness.

trika-school-consummating.short_description: Abhinavagupta's towering synthesis of Trika tantra, recognition philosophy, and rasa-theology.

trika-school-summarizing.short_description: Kṣemarāja's compact, accessible summary (Pratyabhijñā-Hṛdayam) of Pratyabhijñā doctrine.

shabda-advaita.short_description: Bhartṛhari's grammarian thesis that Brahman is essentially śabda; influences Maṇḍana but is not Vedāntic proper.

samkhya-classical.short_description: Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhya-Kārikā: the canonical statement of the twenty-five tattvas, satkārya-vāda, and the three guṇas as constitutive of prakṛti.

samkhya-classical-commentarial.short_description: The early commentary stratum on the Sāṃkhya-Kārikā (Gauḍapāda's Bhāṣya) that fixes the school's reading of the kārikās.

yoga-samkhya-synthesis.short_description: Vyāsa's Yoga-Sūtra-Bhāṣya: Sāṃkhya metaphysics held within the Pātañjala-Yoga frame, with īśvara as a special puruṣa untouched by kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya.

aksapada-nyaya.short_description: Akṣapāda Gautama's Nyāya-Sūtra: the foundational five-adhyāya treatise on the sixteen padārthas, the four pramāṇas, and the five-membered inference.

old-nyaya.short_description: The classical commentary stack — Vātsyāyana's Bhāṣya, Uddyotakara's Vārttika, Vāchaspati's Tātparya-Ṭīkā, Udayana's Pariśuddhi — through which Nyāya develops its mature realist epistemology against the Buddhist pramāṇa-tradition.

navya-nyaya-founding.short_description: Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya's Tattva-Cintāmaṇi: the founding text that resets the standard of inferential rigour through formal vyāpti, avacchedaka, and paryāpti.

navya-nyaya-navadvipa.short_description: The Navadvīpa scholastic stratum that consolidates Navya-Nyāya — Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's Dīdhiti and Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa, which reduces the Vaiśeṣika categories from seven to three.

mimamsa-bhatta.short_description: Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's Mīmāṃsā: the realist-exegetical school whose theory of svataḥ-prāmāṇya and analysis of śabda-pramāṇa stand directly behind Maṇḍana and the Bhāmatī tradition.

mimamsa-prabhakara.short_description: Prabhākara Miśra's Mīmāṃsā: the rival sub-school holding triputī-saṃvit (cognition is jointly self-, object-, and subject-revealing) and the kāryānvitābhidhāna theory of sentence-meaning.

mimamsa-founding-sutra.short_description: Jaimini's Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra: the founding twelve-adhyāya treatise opening with athāto dharma-jijñāsā and establishing codanā (Vedic injunction) as the autonomous pramāṇa for dharma.

mimamsa-bhasyakara.short_description: Śabara Svāmin's Śābara-Bhāṣya: the first surviving major commentary on the Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra, on which both Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara sub-schools build their divergent vārttikas.

patanjala-yoga.short_description: Patañjali's Yoga-Sūtra in four pādas (Samādhi, Sādhana, Vibhūti, Kaivalya): the founding treatise of classical Yoga whose received reading is mediated by the Vyāsa-Bhāṣya commentary tradition.

vaisesika-old.short_description: Kaṇāda's Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra: the founding ten-adhyāya treatise establishing the six padārthas (dravya, guṇa, karma, sāmānya, viśeṣa, samavāya) and the atomistic causal account.

vaisesika-classical.short_description: Praśastapāda's Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha: the prose recasting that becomes the standard exposition of Vaiśeṣika and on which (disputedly with him or with later commentators) the seventh category abhāva is added.

jaina-foundational.short_description: Umāsvāti's Tattvārtha-Sūtra: the cross-sectarian foundational treatise accepted by both Digambara and Śvetāmbara, articulating sarva-jña-doctrine, anekānta-vāda, and syād-vāda.

jaina-digambara.short_description: Kundakunda's Digambara two-truths Jaina (vyavahāra-naya / niścaya-naya): the structural-parallel-to-Advaita whose formal correspondence with vyāvahārika/pāramārthika is engaged in the comparative claims.

jaina-svetambara-doxographer.short_description: Haribhadra Sūri's Śvetāmbara position: author of the Ṣaḍ-Darśana-Samuccaya, the first systematic Indian doxography that engages the rival darśanas with disciplined fairness.

jaina-svetambara-polymath.short_description: Hemacandra Sūri's combination of Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā (Jaina pramāṇa-theory) and Yoga-Śāstra (Jaina yoga praxis) at the high scholastic level of late twelfth-century Gujarat.

lokayata-reconstructed.short_description: The Cārvāka / Lokāyata position as reconstructible from hostile-citation: pratyakṣa as sole pramāṇa, the rejection of inference as evidentially unreliable, the rejection of the post-mortem soul.

madhyamaka-founder.short_description: Nāgārjuna's founding stratum of the middle-way: the Mūla-Madhyamaka-Kārikā with its catuṣkoṭi-driven refutation of svabhāva and the identification of śūnyatā with pratītya-samutpāda (MMK 24.18).

prasangika.short_description: Candrakīrti's Prāsaṅgika Mādhyamaka: refuses to posit any conventional ontology even at the saṃvṛti level, advancing only prasaṅga (consequence) refutations rather than independent svatantra-anumānas.

svatantrika.short_description: Bhāviveka's Svātantrika Mādhyamaka: accepts svatantra-anumāna (independent inference) at the conventional level while preserving Nāgārjuna's ultimate śūnyatā; the polemical pair of Candrakīrti's Prāsaṅgika.

yogacara-founder.short_description: Asaṅga's founding stratum of the cognition-only school: the trisvabhāva analysis (parikalpita / paratantra / pariniṣpanna), the ālaya-vijñāna as transformative substrate, and the systematisation of bodhisattva-praxis in the Bodhisattva-Bhūmi.

sakara-vijnanavada.short_description: The Yogācāra current that holds cognition to bear an internal image-form (ākāra) of its content; the position Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikā develops in defence of vijñapti-mātratā against external-object realism.

nirakara-vijnanavada.short_description: The rival Yogācāra current denying that cognition bears an internal ākāra: cognition is bare luminous awareness without form, the form-bearing reading being a concession to ordinary discourse rather than a metaphysical commitment.

pramana-vada-founder.short_description: Dignāga's founding stratum of the Buddhist epistemological school: the Pramāṇa-Samuccaya, the pramāṇa-dvaya thesis (only pratyakṣa and anumāna), the svalakṣaṇa / sāmānya-lakṣaṇa distinction, and the apoha-vāda of concept-formation.

pramana-vada-mature.short_description: Dharmakīrti's mature Buddhist epistemology: the Pramāṇa-Vārttika and its sub-treatises develop trairūpya, svabhāva-pratibandha as the ground of inferential necessity, the radical kṣaṇika-vāda defence, and the mature apoha apparatus answered by Udayana and Gaṅgeśa.

sarvastivada-vaibhasika.short_description: The orthodox Kashmiri Sarvāstivāda school of the Mahā-Vibhāṣā: the foundational sarvam asti thesis (past, present, and future dharmas exist in their respective temporal modes), defended in Saṅghabhadra's Nyāyānusāra against the Sautrāntika reformist gloss in Vasubandhu's auto-commentary on the Abhidharma-Kośa.

sautrantika.short_description: The reformist Abhidharmic stream that recognises only the present moment as actually existent and treats past and future dharmas as conceptual constructions; articulated in Vasubandhu's auto-commentary (Bhāṣya) on the Abhidharma-Kośa where it functions as internal critique of the Vaibhāṣika orthodoxy carried forward in the verses themselves.

theravada-mahavihara.short_description: The Mahāvihāra-line Pāli Theravāda systematised by Buddhaghosa: the Visuddhimagga as the canonical sevenfold-purification path, the Aṭṭhasālinī as the Abhidhamma commentary, and the Sumaṅgala-Vilāsinī as the Dīgha-Nikāya commentary, collectively fixing the Theravāda doctrinal standard in the fifth century.

tathagatagarbha-foundational.short_description: The foundational Tathāgatagarbha treatises traditionally ascribed to Maitreya (channeled by Asaṅga per the school's account; modern scholarship identifies one or more historical authors): Ratna-Gotra-Vibhāga (Uttara-Tantra-Śāstra), Mahāyāna-Sūtra-Alaṃkāra, Madhyānta-Vibhāga, Dharma-Dharmatā-Vibhāga, Abhisamayālaṃkāra.

buddha-nature-mahayana.short_description: The composite Mahāyāna sūtra-stream that synthesises Yogācāra and Tathāgatagarbha doctrine: the Laṅkāvatāra-Sūtra as the principal source for the eight-vijñāna model, the citta-mātra doctrine, and the introduction of ālaya-vijñāna terminology into the Buddha-nature literature.

kashmir-shaiva-siddhanta-founding.short_description: Sadyojyotis's founding Saiddhāntika treatises (Bhoga-Kārikā, Mokṣa-Kārikā, Tattva-Saṅgraha, Nareśvara-Parīkṣā): pati, paśu, and pāśa as three eternally distinct realities.

kashmir-shaiva-siddhanta-mature.short_description: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II's mature dualist Saiddhāntika commentaries (on the Mataṅga, Kiraṇa, Nareśvara-Parīkṣā): the developed defence of three-fold ontology against the rising Pratyabhijñā non-dualism.

trika-pratyabhijna-founder.short_description: Somānanda's Śiva-Dṛṣṭi: the founding text of the Pratyabhijñā stream, prior to its analytic systematisation in Utpaladeva.

trika-spanda-founding.short_description: Vasugupta's Śiva-Sūtra (and on one tradition the Spanda-Kārikā): the foundational Spanda revelation, with spanda as the dynamism of manifestation as a whole.

trika-spanda-systematic.short_description: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa's Spanda-Sarva-Sva (and on one tradition the Spanda-Kārikā): the first systematic articulation of Spanda doctrine within the Trika lineage.

trika-tantraloka-commentarial.short_description: Jayaratha's Tantrāloka-Viveka: the principal commentary on Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka, indispensable for the mature Trika.

shakta-srividya-early-modern.short_description: Bhāskararāya's Vārāṇasī Śrīvidyā synthesis (Saubhāgya-Bhāskara, Setubandha, Varivasyā-Rahasya): the major early-modern integration of Śrīvidyā ritual-philosophy with Advaita Vedānta.

shakta-srividya-south-indian-smarta.short_description: Lakṣmīdhara's Saundarya-Laharī commentary: the standard South-Indian Smārta-aligned reading of the Saundarya-Laharī, conservative on the samaya / kaula distinction.

shakta-mahartha-source.short_description: Abhinanda Yogin: the Mahārtha-Mañjarī base verses, the principal South-Indian Krama source.

shakta-mahartha-systematic.short_description: Maheśvarānanda's Mahārtha-Mañjarī with the Parimala auto-commentary: the mature South-Indian Krama systematisation.

purna-advaita-integral.short_description: Sri Aurobindo's Pūrṇa-Advaita: Brahman as Saccidānanda whose real self-multiplication through descending grades and evolutionary ascent is affirmed against any reading of Śaṅkara that makes the manifest world ultimately unreal.

ramananda-sampradaya.short_description: Rāma-centric Viśiṣṭādvaita of the Rāmānanda Sampradāya, preserving Rāmānuja's śarīra-śarīrin template with Rāma (Sītā-Rāma) as the parabrahman of every Upaniṣad.

vadakalai-modern-systematic.short_description: Twentieth-century Vaḍakalai consolidation (Uttamūr Vīrarāghavācārya, Abhinava Deśika), integrating Navya-Nyāya logical apparatus into Viśiṣṭādvaita exegesis and defending Vedānta-Deśika's positions against Advaita and Tenkalai criticism.

vadakalai-upanishad-bhashya.short_description: Late-sixteenth- to mid-seventeenth-century Vaḍakalai stream (Raṅgarāmānuja Muni) that produced the school's first Sanskrit Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas, filling the bhāṣya-coverage gap left by Rāmānuja.

vivarana-modern-editorial.short_description: Twentieth-century Vivaraṇa-line modern editorial-polemical Advaita (Anantakṛṣṇa Śāstrī), defending the post-Madhusūdana scholastic apparatus through critical editions of the Advaita-Siddhi / Nyāyāmṛta exchange and polemical treatises against Viśiṣṭādvaita and Holenarsipur revisionism.

vivriti-pratyabhijna-advaita.short_description: Satchidānandendra Sarasvatī's Śuddha-Śāṅkara-Prakriyā: post-Śaṅkara Advaita scholasticism (mūlāvidyā, Bhāmatī, Vivaraṇa) is read against the adhyāsa-bhāṣya method as a deviation from Śaṅkara's own bhāṣya-corpus.

tattvavada-modern.short_description: Twentieth- and early-twenty-first-century philological-textual Dvaita (Bannañje Govindācārya), re-establishing Madhva's full Sarva-Mūla-Granthāḥ as a critically-edited corpus and supplying modern Sanskrit vyākhyānas across the school's canon.

gaudiya-matha.short_description: Bhaktisiddhānta Sarasvatī's modern systematic-institutional Gauḍīya tradition, codifying acintya-śakti through the Anubhāṣya on the Caitanya-Caritāmṛta and the Bhāgavata-commentary, founding the Gauḍīya Maṭha (1920) as the institutional vehicle.

dashanami-sarasvati-revivalism.short_description: Twentieth-century Daśanāmī-Sarasvatī revivalist Advaita (Karpātrī), combining conservative orthodox Vedānta with vigorous public defense of varṇāśrama-dharma and sanātana-dharma against modern reformism.

shringeri-patta.short_description: Modern Sringeri-paṭṭa Advaita continuity (Candraśekhara Bhāratī III, jagadguru 1912–1954), preserving the Vivaraṇa-Bhāmatī-synthesis curriculum of the Sringeri Śāradā Pīṭha as living institutional tradition.

pasupata-foundational.short_description: The foundational Pāśupata stratum: Lakulīśa's Pāśupata-Sūtra in five chapters (vidhi, dvāra, yoga, vidhi-niṣedha, duḥkha-anta) and Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya, articulating the pati-paśu-pāśa triad and Śiva as nimitta-kāraṇa.

pancaratra-tantric.short_description: The Vaiṣṇava-Tantric Pāñcarātra tradition as such: the four canonical Saṃhitās (Sātvata, Pauṣkara, Jayākhya, Ahirbudhnya) and the Lakṣmī-Tantra, with vyūha, śuddha-sattva, and vibhava as the load-bearing doctrines refuted at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.42–45.

pancaratra-defended-by-yamuna.short_description: The Pāñcarātra-tradition as defended-from-within Viśiṣṭādvaita: Yāmuna's Āgama-Prāmāṇya re-establishes the canonical authority of the Saṃhitās against Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.42–45, and Vedānta-Deśika's Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā continues the defence.

virashaiva-aniruddha-tradition.short_description: The twelfth-century Karnataka Vīraśaiva movement of Basavaṇṇa and the Śaraṇas (Allama Prabhu, Akkamahādevī, Cennabasava): vacana-literature in Old-Kannada, iṣṭa-liṅga worship, the aṣṭāvaraṇa, and pañcācāra; the lineage that traces itself through the aniruddha-vyūha to Śiva.

bhairava-tantra-systematic.short_description: The wider Bhairava-tantra textual tradition (Mālinī-Vijaya-Tantra, Siddha-Yogeśvarī-Mata, Rudra-Yāmala, Triśirobhairava) systematised in the Kashmirian commentarial tradition; the corpus Abhinavagupta engages as scripture.

trika-source-tantras.short_description: The specific subset of Bhairava-tantras Abhinavagupta singles out as the scriptural basis of Trika: the Mālinī-Vijaya as foundational, with the Siddha-Yogeśvarī-Mata and the Triśirobhairava as auxiliary; the Mālinī-Vijaya-Vārttika and Tantrāloka are the principal interpretive vehicles.

digambara-dialectical.short_description: Akalaṅka's Digambara dialectical stratum: Aṣṭa-Sahasrī, Tattvārtha-Vārttika (Rāja-Vārttika), Laghīyastraya, Nyāya-Viniścaya, Pramāṇa-Saṅgraha, and Siddhi-Viniścaya develop Jain pramāṇa-theory and anekānta-vāda against Dharmakīrti-school Buddhists and Brahmanical opponents.

svetambara-navya-nyaya-engaged.short_description: The early-modern Śvetāmbara stratum (Yaśovijaya Upādhyāya, 1624–1688) that engages Navya-Nyāya in its own technical idiom and produces a Jaina-Navya-Nyāya synthesis: Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā, Nyāya-Loka, Adhyātma-Sāra, and the commentary on Hemacandra's Pramāṇa-Mīmāṃsā.

svetambara-syadvada-systematic.short_description: Malliṣeṇa's Syādvāda-Mañjarī, the canonical thirteenth-century commentary on Hemacandra's Anya-Yoga-Vyavaccheda-Dvātriṃśikā, that exposes anekānta-vāda as a meta-philosophical position covering all rival schools' positive theses.
